nativeRange.habitat: Rocky substrates and coral reef flats in the photic zone; forms stiff, hairy tufts on hard surfaces such as live rock and coral skeleton.

avoidWith: herbivorous fish that may sample it

imageCandidates.notes: Mudasir Zainuddin; 4000x3000. Sharp, vibrant bright-green hairy tuft, complete and centered on rock in situ — textbook Turtle Weed growth form. Best diagnostic and quality match.
Henrik Kibak; 2048x2025. In-situ green tuft beside Acropora on the reef; shows the stiff hairy filaments and reef context clearly. Slight blue water cast.
Dr. Robert Ricker, NOAA/NOS/ORR; 3000x1987. Public-domain close-up of the fine green filaments (with pale zooidangia tips); good filament-detail variety, tighter framing than a whole-tuft shot.

summary: Turtle Weed (Chlorodesmis fastigiata) is a stiff, hairy-tufted green macroalgae native to Indo-Pacific reefs. It produces a chemical toxin that tends to deter herbivores but can stress adjacent corals. It is a specialty species generally considered advanced to keep.
careNotes: High-intensity, full-spectrum lighting is commonly recommended; actinic-only spectrum is generally considered insufficient for this and similarly demanding species. Flow should be moderate to strong — strong current facilitates waste removal and respiration. Turtle Weed produces chlorodesmin, a toxic diterpene compound that tends to deter most herbivorous fish. However, the same toxin may stress or inhibit corals placed in direct contact, so a gap should be maintained between clumps and adjacent coral colonies. The semi-aggressive temperament rating reflects this allelopathic chemical production rather than any physical aggression. Weekly dosing of iron, iodine, and other trace elements is generally advised. As a green macroalgae that attaches via a holdfast, Turtle Weed should be secured to rock by wedging, zip-tie, fishing line, or a similar method — do not bury the base in sand. Turtle Weed is rarely available in the US aquarium trade and is sourced primarily through specialty vendors. No dissolved copper should be present in the system.
breedingNotes: Propagation in captivity is most reliably achieved by simple fragmentation — breaking off a clump and attaching it to a new rock or hard surface via wedging or zip-tie. Sporulation has been documented in natural populations but is not commonly used in captive settings.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros — Turtle Weed
primary.notes: Primary trade source. Confirms species identity (Chlorodesmis fastigiata), growth form (hairy tufts), and non-invasive nature. Product page has minimal numerical parameters; supplemented by secondary sources.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros — Macroalgae Care Guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source. Provides canonical parameters: temp 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, Ca 400–450 ppm, Mg 1250–1350 ppm, NO3 5–20 ppm, PO4 0.02–0.1 ppm. Also confirms attachment methods and sand-bed guidance.

additional.name: AquariumDomain — Turtle Weed Species Profile
additional.notes: Cross-reference for C. fastigiata specifically. Provided temp 72–81°F, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, high lighting, expert care level, propagation by fragmentation/sporulation, max 8 in height, chlorodesmin toxin deterrence noted.

additional.name: Marine Plant Book — Turtle Weed
additional.notes: Second cross-reference for genus Chlorodesmis. Confirmed high lighting, 72–80°F, advanced care level, moderate growth, 8 in max height, native range Australia/Eastern Caribbean/S. Pacific, depth ~3–15 m, good nutrient uptake, allelopathic toxin affecting coral growth.
